title: CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 — Audit record creation verification on CUI systems
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the CMMC programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with CMMC control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SPL directly verifies audit record creation on CUI systems — the core requirement of AU.L2-3.3.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 (Create audit records) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.20.3: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 3.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.20.3' running on WinEventLog:Security, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 — Audit record creation verification on CUI' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper CMMC control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the CMMC control objective.

dataSources: All indexes feeding CUI-classified hosts, `cui_hosts.csv` lookup, `cui_required_sources.csv` lookup
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). (Splunkbase)
spl:
| tstats count WHERE index=* by index, sourcetype, host _time span=1h
| lookup cui_hosts.csv host OUTPUT cui_flag
| where cui_flag="true"
| stats dc(sourcetype) AS sourcetype_count dc(host) AS host_count latest(_time) AS last_event BY index
| lookup cui_required_sources.csv index OUTPUT required_sourcetypes
| eval missing=if(sourcetype_count < required_sourcetypes, "gap", "ok")
| where missing="gap"
| table index, sourcetype_count, required_sourcetypes, host_count, last_event
description: Verifies that all required audit record sources (Windows Security, EDR, network flow, application logs) are actively ingesting from CUI-classified systems. CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 requires organisations to create and retain audit records to the extent needed to enable monitoring, analysis, investigation, and reporting of unlawful or unauthorized system activity.
value: Provides continuous verification that audit record creation is functioning on all CUI-scoped systems. Identifies gaps in log collection that would violate the CMMC requirement to create audit records for all CUI system activity.
implementation: (1) Verify data sources: All indexes feeding CUI-classified hosts, `cui_hosts.csv` lookup, `cui_required_sources.csv` lookup; (2) Maintain CUI-scoped lookups; (3) Schedule search at compliance-aligned interval; (4) Route findings to ticketing queue; (5) Retain exports per records management policy.
visualization: Table showing index, expected vs actual sourcetype count, host count, and last event time. Single value panel for total gaps detected.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `cui_hosts.csv`, `cui_required_sources.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('cui_hosts.csv','cui_required_sources.csv')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `AU.L2-3.3.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 (Create audit records) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.20.3: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 3." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Verify data sources: All indexes feeding CUI-classified hosts, `cui_hosts.csv` lookup, `cui_required_sources.csv` lookup. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** Maintain CUI-scoped lookups. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule search at compliance-aligned interval. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route findings to ticketing queue. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Retain exports per records management policy. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 — Audit record creation verification on CUI systems," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the CMMC control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `dc()` is grouped by `index, sourcetype, host, _time`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.20.3: CMMC AU.L2-3.3.1 — Audit record creation verification on CUI]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.20.3,reg=CMMC
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `index`, `sourcetype`, `host`, `cui_flag`, `required_sourcetypes`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table index, sourcetype, host, cui_flag, required_sourcetypes
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `cui_hosts.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup cui_hosts.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.20.3: CMMC evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Showing Index (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Expected Vs Actual Sourcetype Count (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Host Count (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — And Last Event Time. Single Value Panel For Total Gaps Detected (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CMMC-specific failure modes:**

• Level 1/2/3 distinction: CMMC 2.0 has three levels (Foundational/Advanced/Expert). Detections must specify level — Level 1 is self-assessed against basic cyber hygiene; Level 3 requires NIST SP 800-172 enhanced controls plus triennial assessment.

• FCI vs CUI boundary: CMMC applies only to systems handling Federal Contract Information (FCI) or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI). Detections scoped to 'entire organisation' fire on out-of-boundary systems, wasting audit effort.

• POA&M acceptance limitation: unlike FISMA, CMMC limits POA&M use — certain controls cannot have open POA&M items at assessment. Detections must know which controls can have open POA&M and which cannot.

• C3PAO assessment cadence: Level 2+ requires third-party assessment every 3 years. Detections measuring control stability must span the full triennial cycle; short-window detections miss drift between assessments.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for AU.L2-3.3.1 — Audit record creation verification on CUI systems. CMMC sets the bar for protecting sensitive information, and we keep evidence for the level we target.
